ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mirchi Pakora; mapped to the nearest fritter class. Mirchi Pakora is a deep-fried gram-flour-battered green chilli fritter.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered snacks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Mirchi Pakora entry; A0CKP (fritters/deep-fried battered snacks) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Mirchi Pakora or equivalent chilli fritter.

allergens: gluten (gram flour may be cross-contaminated; primary batter is gram flour which is gluten-free but cross-contamination risk exists)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mirchi Pakora is 2–3 pieces weighing approximately 80 g, commonly served as a snack or side dish at Indian and Singaporean hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Mirchi Pakora — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Mirchi Pakora (cr_id G00011029). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central gram-flour fritter analogue (USDA SR Legacy item 'Fritters, plain') as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chickpea flour / besan-based foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mirchi Pakora in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the closest analogous food: gram-flour (besan) based fritter / pakora, which has a reported GI of approximately 50–55 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (16.24 g × 0.80 serving fraction = 12.99 g) / 100 = 6.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 380 mg/100 g is at the upper borderline of the moderate range (200–400 mg/100 g); potassium at 214 mg/100 g partially offsets sodium but does not fully mitigate risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mirchi Pakora to small portions (≤80 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as the 380 mg/100 g sodium content approaches the high-concern threshold; the 214 mg/100 g potassium provides partial mitigation but is insufficient to offset sodium at larger servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Gram-flour (besan) batter confers a low GI (~52), and dietary fibre at 3.2 g/100 g moderates postprandial glucose response; however, deep-frying increases caloric density and total fat, requiring portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mirchi Pakora has a low estimated GI (~52) due to gram-flour batter and 3.2 g/100 g dietary fibre, which slows glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 80 g per serving and monitor total carbohydrate intake (16.2 g/100 g) to avoid cumulative glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; sodium at 380 mg/100 g is borderline high; however, cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g and saturated fat is low (~0.6 g/100 g), partially mitigating cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mirchi Pakora in moderation (≤80 g per occasion) due to its deep-fried preparation increasing total fat and caloric density, and its borderline sodium content of 380 mg/100 g; the absence of cholesterol and low saturated fat are favourable factors but do not offset the overall risk profile of regular deep-fried snack consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; green chillies are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may exacerbate RA symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Mirchi Pakora in its deep-fried form due to AGE generation and nightshade (solanine) content from green chillies; an air-fried or baked preparation would substantially reduce pro-inflammatory risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mirchi Pakora (also spelled Mirchi Pakoda, Hindi: मिर्ची पकोड़ा, Tamil: மிர்ச்சி பகோரா) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made from whole green chillies coated in a spiced gram-flour (besan) batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed across India, Pakistan, and in the South Asian diaspora communities of Singapore and Malaysia. The exterior is crunchy and golden, while the interior chilli provides a sharp, pungent heat balanced by the earthy, slightly nutty flavour of the besan batter. Spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli powder are typically incorporated into the batter. Mirchi Pakora is commonly served as a street food snack, tea-time accompaniment, or starter at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 125 kcal, 4.92 g protein, 4.77 g fat, 16.24 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. The gram-flour batter confers a low estimated glycaemic index (~52), making it more favourable than wheat-flour-based fritters for blood glucose management. However, deep-frying increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. The high sodium content (380 mg/100 g) warrants caution for individuals with hypertension. Mirchi Pakora is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: Is mirchi pakora good for diabetics?
Can I eat mirchi pakora with high blood pressure?
What is mirchi pakoda made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011029, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy fritter analogue and documented. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous gram-flour fritter data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean-context dish are present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain gram-flour fritter, as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Mirchi Pakora are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based deep-fried snack with no animal-derived ingredients). 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on gram-flour (besan) fritter data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Mirchi Pakora exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 80 g serving size: GL = 52 × (16.24 × 0.80) / 100 = 6.7. 6. FoodOn URI mapped to fritter class (closest_parent match); FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as nearest defensible category anchor for deep-fried battered snacks (closest_parent match).
